title: Measuring and adjusting mains water pressure
subtitle: How to test with a pressure gauge, what the numbers mean, and when a pressure reducing valve is needed
excerpt: UK mains water pressure typically arrives at 2-6 bar at the boundary stop tap. Pressure below 1 bar causes poor shower flow; above 3 bar it stresses appliance seals and causes water hammer. A pressure gauge test takes five minutes. A pressure reducing valve (PRV) installation brings high pressure into the safe 1.5-2.5 bar operating range.
sourceNotes: WRAS (Water Regulations Advisory Scheme) Water Supply Regulations 1999 for minimum pressure requirements. CIPHE guidance on water pressure in domestic installations. Ofwat consumer pressure standards (minimum 1 bar at the point of delivery, typical range 2-6 bar). BS EN 1567 for pressure reducing valve specifications.

content.text: Water pressure is the force at which water arrives at your taps and appliances, measured in bar (1 bar = 10 m of water head = approximately 14.5 psi). Most UK properties receive mains water at 2-6 bar. The Water Industry Act 1991 requires water companies to maintain a minimum static pressure of 1 bar at the boundary stop tap. Above 3 bar, pipework seals, appliance solenoid valves, and hose connectors wear more quickly, and the risk of water hammer (banging pipes) increases.
Attach a pressure gauge to the outside tap or any accessible threaded cold water tap. Screw-thread gauges that fit standard garden tap threads (3/4 inch BSP) cost around a modest cost.

Turn all other taps and appliances off so no water is flowing in the system (static pressure reading). Open the tap with the gauge fully. The gauge needle will settle at the static pressure, typically within 10 seconds.

Note the static reading. Then open a second tap inside (for example, the kitchen cold tap) and read the pressure again with flow running (dynamic pressure). The difference indicates the pipe friction losses in the property.
What to do with different readings
Below 1 bar static: contact your water company. Low incoming pressure is a supply fault, not a household plumbing problem. The company has a legal obligation to deliver minimum 1 bar at the boundary.
1-3 bar: normal operating range. No action needed.

Above 3 bar: fit a pressure reducing valve (PRV) at the incoming main inside the property (usually near the stop tap under the kitchen sink). A standard PRV is set to 2.0-2.5 bar and costs a modest cost for the valve; a plumber charges around a modest cost to install.

A PRV is installed on the incoming cold water main, after the internal stop tap and before the branch to the kitchen tap. It is adjustable: a lockshield screw on the top sets the downstream pressure. Most domestic PRVs have an integral gauge so you can set and verify the output pressure directly. The installation requires isolating the water supply, cutting the pipe, and soldering or using push-fit fittings either side of the PRV body. A competent DIYer can fit it; it is not notifiable work under Building Regulations.
